formatted_summary:
agent2 (Round 2 summarized): The available literature does not demonstrate that single-agent mitoxantrone matches the efficacy of combination chemotherapy in this population—standard care favors multimodal treatments due to their stronger outcomes across multiple endpoints including progression-free survival and overall survival.

Answer Choice: \boxed{\text{B}}

The only source supporting use of mitoxantrone was its role in second-line after prior anthracyline exposure—a setting where benefit may be limited compared to initial combinations involving taxanes plus alkylating agents such as those seen in TAC regimen.

No direct comparisons exist regarding first-line monotherapy versus active combination strategies within high-risk metastatic populations.

Therefore, despite shared mechanism via topo-II inhibition among some drugs, there’s insufficient proof linking single-agent mitoxantrone directly to comparable results against modern combinatorial frontlines.

Thus, our position stands firm—the absence of supportive trial-level data makes option A implausible while C introduces uncertainty without grounding in actual findings.

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{B}}
Only consider information presented during tool calls and discussions above unless instructed otherwise.

Based solely upon the retrieved documentation:

There is no evidence indicating that first-line single-agent mitoxantrone achieves similar efficacy to combination chemotherapy in high-risk metastatic breast cancer. Standard guidelines favor multitherapy regimens over single-agent anthracyclines due to better prognostic outcomes observed clinically. 

\boxed{B}

Mitoxantrone has been studied primarily in later lines of therapy after anthracycline failure, not as a frontline strategy. Combination chemotherapies remain the gold standard owing to robust outcome data from randomized controlled trials.  

\boxed{B}. Answer choice: B
